summary: Joel discusses two mundane office topics: choosing a firewall appliance and dealing with aggressive telemarketing from MCI. The bulk of the post focuses on his frustrating experience with MCI telemarketers who exploited a legal loophole requiring customers to use the exact phrase 'please put me on your do-not-call list' before they would stop calling. Joel questions the business logic of deliberately annoying potential customers and promotes New York State's do-not-call registry as a solution.
key_points: MCI telemarketers were trained to ignore removal requests unless the customer used the legally exact phrase 'please put me on your do-not-call list'
Joel questions the management logic behind policies that deliberately antagonize potential customers
New York State offered a do-not-call web form that could result in $2000 fines for violators
The post captures a pre-national-Do-Not-Call-Registry moment in telemarketing regulation history
money_quotes: Hi, this is your local phone company,' they lied.
Apparently the telemarketers are now being trained that unless they hear this magic phrase word for word, they should keep calling you.
Why do they want to call me if they know it makes me angry? What kind of company would do that?
It can't possibly help and can only hurt.
If enough people (and states) figure this out, maybe one day telemarketing will be worthless and nobody will bother any more.
key_insight: Companies that optimize for exploiting legal loopholes to keep annoying customers are engaging in self-defeating management logic that can only damage their brand.
